Col. Loveland says, however
that he received no rebates or spe-
cial rates from the s. P. He ex-
plains that the rate given him by
the railroad, though lower than
supposedly received by other ship
pers, was a competitive one. At
that time, Loveland declares. he
was shipping most Of his goods by
water, and told the s. p. If 1t would
make him a rate as low AS the
water transportation companies he
would ship by rail. Loveland states
that the rate was granted, but that
1t was not a secret or illegitimate
one.


Gov. Gillett when asked If he
knew that Loveland had received
concessions from the S. p. when
the latter was appointed said no.

class man."


Interstate Commerce Commits
sioner Franklin X. Lane. who is
now in San Francisco, declined
either to deny or affirm the exist
ence of evidence against Col. Love
land. AS the matter would come
under the jurisdiction of the attor-
ney general of California. the in
terstate commission will take no
further action in any event


Transcript Of evidence showing
the guilt of the S. p. of giving re-
bates within the state Of California
has been received by the California
railroad commission and some &C.
ton must be taken regarding 1t at
the commission meeting early in
January. A railroad commissioner
who declares himself in favor OF
prosecuting the S. p. says however.
that the state Of California should
provide more funds for it's railroad
commissioners. Only $500 yearly
or just about enough to pay for
printing. stationery and such minor
eXpenses-is allowed the commits
sion. while other states--Illinois,
for instance-have funds as high
as $4,000 set aside for the use of
their railroad commissions,
